coredata.dc:title: Optimization of acoustic parameters for ultrasonic separation of emulsions with different physical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Xiaoming
Gong, Haiyang
Yin, Haoran
He, Ziling
He, Limin
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasound is an emerging and promising method for demulsification, which is highly affected by acoustic parameters and emulsion properties. Herein, a series of microscopic and dehydration experiments are carried out to investigate the parameter optimization of ultrasonic separation. The results show that the optimal acoustic parameters highly depend on the emulsion properties. For low frequency ultrasonic standing waves (USWs), mechanical vibrations not only facilitate droplet collision and coalescence, but also disperse the surfactant absorbed on the interface to decrease the interfacial strength. Therefore, low frequency ultrasound is suitable for separating emulsions with high viscosity and high interfacial strength. Increasing the energy density to produce moderate cavitation can increase demulsification efficiency. However, excessive cavitation results in secondary emulsification. In high frequency USWs, the droplets migrate directionally and form bandings, thereby promoting droplet coalescence. Therefore, high frequency ultrasound is favorable for separating emulsions with low dispersed phase content and small droplet size. Increasing the energy density can accelerate the aggregation of droplets, however, excessive energy density causes acoustic streaming that disturbs the aggregated droplets, resulting in reduced demulsification efficiency. This work presents rules for acoustic parameter optimization, further advancing industrial applications of ultrasonic separation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Emulsion separation
Acoustic parameters
Droplet banding
Mechanical vibration
Ultrasonic standing waves

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasonic emulsion separation for different acoustic parameters
Effect of frequency on emulsion separation
Effect of energy density on emulsion separation
Effect of treatment time on emulsion separation
Ultrasonic emulsion separation for different emulsion properties
Effect of continuous phase viscosity on emulsion separation
Effect of interfacial tension on emulsion separation
Effect of dispersed phase content on emulsion separation

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the experimental setup for ultrasonic demulsification.
Sketch of emulsion separation in USWs. The red sine line represents the primary acoustic force F 1, ac(y), while the blue sine line represents the acoustic pressure P (y).
Demulsification efficiency vs. energy density at varying frequencies.
Coalescence evolution of the droplets near the cavitation bubbles in low frequency USWs (f = 25.8 kHz, e = 17.7 J·ml−1).
Droplets aggregate and form bandings in high frequency USWs (f = 126.4 kHz, e = 37.2 J·ml−1).
Demulsification efficiency vs. treatment time at 25.8 and 126.4 kHz.
Demulsification efficiency vs. energy density at varying continuous phase viscosities (T r = 10 min, γ = 7.14 mN·m−1, φ = 10%).
Relative demulsification efficiency vs. continuous phase viscosity at 25.8 and 126.4 kHz.
Demulsification efficiency vs. energy density at varying interfacial tensions (T r = 10 min, μ = 106 mPa·s, φ = 10%).
Relative demulsification efficiency vs. interfacial tension at 25.8 and 126.4 kHz.
Demulsification efficiency vs. energy density at varying dispersed phase contents (T r = 10 min, μ = 106 mPa·s, γ = 7.14 mN·m−1).
Relative demulsification efficiency vs. dispersed phase content at 25.8 and 126.4 kHz.

head.ce:title: Optimization of acoustic parameters for ultrasonic separation of emulsions with different physical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oil & Gas Storage and Transportation Safety, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oil & Gas Storage and Transportation Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oil & Gas Storage and Transportation Safety, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound is an emerging and promising method for demulsification, which is highly affected by acoustic parameters and emulsion properties. Herein, a series of microscopic and dehydration experiments are carried out to investigate the parameter optimization of ultrasonic separation. The results show that the optimal acoustic parameters highly depend on the emulsion properties. For low frequency ultrasonic standing waves (USWs), mechanical vibrations not only facilitate droplet collision and coalescence, but also disperse the surfactant absorbed on the interface to decrease the interfacial strength. Therefore, low frequency ultrasound is suitable for separating emulsions with high viscosity and high interfacial strength. Increasing the energy density to produce moderate cavitation can increase demulsification efficiency. However, excessive cavitation results in secondary emulsification. In high frequency USWs, the droplets migrate directionally and form bandings, thereby promoting droplet coalescence. Therefore, high frequency ultrasound is favorable for separating emulsions with low dispersed phase content and small droplet size. Increasing the energy density can accelerate the aggregation of droplets, however, excessive energy density causes acoustic streaming that disturbs the aggregated droplets, resulting in reduced demulsification efficiency. This work presents rules for acoustic parameter optimization, further advancing industrial applications of ultrasonic separation.

ce:section.ce:para: A variety of emulsions with complex compositions are commonplace in petroleum industry, chemical engineering, food processing and other fields. In order to effectively separate emulsions, many demulsification methods have been developed, including adding demulsifiers, centrifugation, filtration, electricity, ultrasound, microwaves, and others [1–6]. Among these methods, ultrasonic treatment has received considerable attention in recent years since it is low cost, pollution-free and highly efficient. It has a wide scope of applications, e.g. crude oil dehydration [7], whole milk degreasing [8], vegetable oil extraction [9] and sludge deoiling [10]. Moreover, ultrasound possesses great prospects in the area of enhanced oil recovery (EOR) because of its significant viscosity reduction effect [11]. Electromagnetic waves with RF (Radio Frequencies), which set polar molecules into high frequency collision and produce much heat, can be applied to deal with the high viscosity of heavy oil [12]. However, its main drawback is the limited penetration depth of microwaves in conductive mediums [13]. Ultrasonic-based EOR may be a good alternative since the energy dissipation is not heavy when ultrasound penetrates into the oil–water mixture [14].

Acoustic parameters, particularly frequency and acoustic intensity, are key factors in determining demulsification efficiency [15]. The applied frequency is closely related to the physical properties of the emulsion. In general, low frequency ultrasound (20–50 kHz) is used for crude oil dehydration, and droplet coagulation is considered to be a dominant factor for emulsion separation [16]. Antes et al. [17] showed that the dehydration efficiency of the crude oil emulsion reaches a maximum at 45 kHz, and that it is difficult to separate water from emulsions when the frequency exceeds 130 kHz. In food processing, high frequency ultrasound from 0.4 to 10 MHz is usually used to separate biomass particles [9]. In order to separate micron-sized particles, the applied frequency should be higher than 1 MHz, and the smaller the particles, the higher the frequency required [18]. A large number of experiments have shown that MHz ultrasound can achieve better extraction of coconut oil and degreasing of whole milk compared with low frequency ultrasound [19]. Nii et al. [5] showed that the droplets rapidly aggregate and flocculate into clusters in 2 MHz ultrasonic standing waves (USWs).

Optimizing acoustic intensity is essential for efficient demulsification. At low frequencies, increasing acoustic intensity can increase the dehydration efficiency of crude oil emulsions [7]. Once sufficient energy is delivered to the surface of droplets, the barrier between the droplets is broken, causing them to coalesce into larger droplets [20]. However, emulsification occurs when the acoustic intensity increases to the cavitation threshold [21]. At high frequencies, higher acoustic intensity enhances the aggregation of dispersed particles and causes rapid stratification with the continuous phase [22]. However, excessive acoustic intensity can induce acoustic streaming, which disturbs the agglomerated droplets and reduces demulsification efficiency [23]. Therefore, the general consensus is that optimal acoustic intensities exist for separating specific physical emulsions.

Increasing the treatment time facilitates flocculation and coalescence of the droplets, thereby improving demulsification efficiency [15]. In industrial production, it is desirable to achieve efficient demulsification in the shortest possible time. Therefore, many studies have focused on optimizing treatment time to find the most economical solution. Based on normalized analysis, Nii et al. [5] found that improved separation efficiency can be achieved by using long time low-power processing at constant energy consumption. In addition, the optimal treatment time is significantly affected by the physical properties of the emulsion. For example, Mohsin and Meribout [24] showed that the demulsification efficiency of ultrasonic treatment is as weak as that of gravitational sedimentation when the initial water content is more than 50%. In summary, numerous ultrasonic demulsification studies put emphasis on emulsions with specific physical properties, and it is difficult to obtain universal conclusions to guide industrial applications.

Herein, the optimization of ultrasonic separation is studied for varying acoustic parameters and emulsion properties. The optimal frequency, acoustic intensity and treatment time of the emulsions with different viscosities, interfacial tensions and dispersed phase contents are obtained, providing guidance for further industrial applications of ultrasonic separation.

The experimental setup for ultrasonic demulsification is shown in Fig. 1 . Four piezoelectric transducers (rated power: 60 W) were used in the experiments. For each frequency, the ultrasound was generated with a corresponding piezoelectric transducer, which was excited by the high frequency power amplifier (operating frequency: 5–150 kHz, maximum output voltage: 600 Vp-p, maximum output current: 2 Ap-p, Huiyue HFVA62) and function generator (DDS, maximum output frequency: 400 MHz, Rigol DG2041A). An impedance analyzer (Bandera PV50A) was used to measure the resonant frequencies of four different transducers (25.8, 39.4, 90.0 and 126.4 kHz). In order to maximize the energy conversion efficiency of the transducer, the frequency of the amplifier output was set to the resonant frequency of the transducer. With increasing frequency, the diameters of the transducers are 55, 46, 42 and 35 mm, respectively. Transducers were mounted at the bottom of the acoustic chamber (dimensions: 60 mm length, 60 mm width and 80 mm height) made of Perspex. In order to achieve a standing wave field at each frequency, the height from the incident surface to the reflective surface was adjusted to an integral number of 1/2 wavelength. The acoustic signals were measured by scanning in the acoustic chamber with a hydrophone (dimensions: 6 mm diameter, 500 mm length, operating frequency: 20 Hz-200 kHz, sensitivity: −210 dB, CAS CS-3B), visualized with the oscilloscope and recorded by the computer. The formation of ultrasonic standing wave field was verified by measuring the distribution of acoustic pressure in the chamber. Moreover, the energy density was calculated according to e = PT r/V [25], where P is the active electric power on the transducer; T r is the treatment time; V is the volume of the emulsion. At frequencies of 25.8, 39.4 and 90.0 kHz, the active electric power on the transducer was measured using a power meter (maximum input frequency: 100 kHz, HIOKI 3332). At frequencies of 126.4 kHz, the active electric power on the transducer was measured with a high-frequency current probe (maximum input frequency: 20 MHz, maximum peak current: 300 A, Tektronix TRCP0300) and a high voltage probe (maximum input frequency: 500 MHz, maximum peak voltage: 2500 V, Tektronix P5100A). Ultrasonic treatment may result in rise of the transducer temperature, which affects the resonant frequency and results in the decrease of useful power consumption. In order to minimize experimental errors caused by the temperature rise of the transducer during ultrasonic treatment, the useful power consumption was kept constant by adjusting the high frequency power amplifier. The microscopic behavior of the droplets was recorded with a high-speed camera (Phantom v2512) equipped with a 200 × lens (Mitutoyo). The frame rate and resolution were 1000 frames per second and 1280 × 800 pixels, respectively. A LED light source (Olympus LFP-60WP) was used to improve the exposure of the camera.

The ultra-pure water containing varying concentrations of Triton X-100 (Nonionic surfactant) was utilized as the dispersed phase. Silicone oils of different viscosities (52.19, 106.00, 169.50 and 282.17 mPa·s at 20 °C) were used as the continuous phase, respectively. The oil–water interfacial tensions were 4.79, 5.58, 7.14 and 10.35 mN m−1, respectively. The silicone oil viscosities and the oil–water interfacial tensions were measured by a stress-controlled rheometer (Anton Paar MCR 301) and Wilhelmy plate method with an interfacial tensiometer (K100, KRÜSS GmbH - Germany), respectively. Additionally, the droplets were stained with Erioglaucine disodium salt to distinguish between water droplets and the continuous phase.

Different proportions of silicone oil and surfactant aqueous solution were agitated by an IKA agitator to form water-in-oil emulsions with water content of 2.5%, 5%, 10% and 20%, respectively. The size distribution of water droplets was analyzed by the Malvern particle size analyzer. Sauter mean diameter of the dispersed phase was maintained at about 30 μm by adjusting the agitation rate and time.

The water-in-oil emulsion was transferred to the acoustic chamber and treated with ultrasound. With increasing frequency, the volumes of emulsions are 201.6, 176.4, 172.8 and 180.0 ml, respectively. During this process, the microscopic features of the droplets were recorded by the high-speed camera. The experiments were conducted at room temperature (20 °C). For extended ultrasonication times, the dissipation of energy results in heating of the emulsions. In our experiments, the maximum temperature of emulsions after ultrasound treatment is 23.4 °C. After a period of treatment, the emulsion was immersed into the circulating water bath at a constant temperature of 40 °C for 10 min to evaluate the demulsification efficiency η = V ws/V we, where V ws is the volume of water separated from the emulsion; V we is the volume of water in the original emulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonic emulsion separation for different acoustic parameters
Effect of frequency on emulsion separation
ce:section.ce:para: The demulsification process experiences three stages: droplet coalescence, droplet sedimentation or buoyancy, and final phase separation. Fig. 2 shows the emulsion separation principle of USWs. The primary acoustic force F 1, a drives dispersed droplets to migrate to nodal planes, which is the transient phase of the droplet trajectory [26]. The droplet migration speed is determined by acoustic intensity, continuous phase viscosity and droplet size [27]. Subsequently, the droplets stabilize at the positions where the primary acoustic force F 1, ac is equal to the net gravity F g, which is the stready phase of the droplet trajectory . In the process, the droplets aggregate and form bandings with an interval of λ/2 [27]. When the droplets get close to each other, the secondary acoustic force F 2, ac begins to be significant and facilitates droplet agglomeration and coalescence [21]. Eventually, the coalesced droplets gradually settle by the net gravity force.

The time-averaged primary acoustic force is given by [28]:
(1)
F 1,ac
= 4 π k
r 3
E ac
K s
sin
2 k z
where k is the wavenumber inversely proportional to the wave length λ; E ac is the specific energy density proportional to the acoustic intensity I; r is the radius of the droplet; z is the distance from the nodal plane to the droplet; and K s is the acoustic contrast factor.

The distance between the reflecting surface and the radiating surface is set to an integral number of one-half wavelength, enabling the formation of a standing wave. However, no droplet bandings are observed at frequencies of 25.8 and 39.4 kHz. According to Eq. (2), the decrease of the frequency results in greater amplitude of the acoustic wave. The increase of the acoustic wave amplitude is beneficial to the oscillation of droplets with the continuous phase, which prevents the droplets from migrating towards the pressure node. As the frequency increases to 90.0 and 126.4 kHz, the vibration of the droplets is significantly weakened. Therefore, the droplets can migrate directionally and aggregate into bandings at high frequencies.

The amplitude of the acoustic wave A in water-in-oil emulsions is expressed as [29]:
(2)
A
=
I
c o
2
π f
ρ o
where c o is the sound velocity in oil; f is the frequency and ρ o is the oil density.

The key to demulsification is the coalescence of droplets. However, when the droplets approach each other, their coalescence is impeded by the interface film. Therefore, the interface film that stabilizes emulsions should be broken to achieve efficient separation. As shown in Fig. 3 , the optimal demulsification efficiencies at 25.8 and 39.4 kHz are significantly higher than that at 90.0 and 126.4 kHz. Power ultrasound (20–100 kHz) can transmit high specific energy density to the medium and produces strong mechanical vibrations [30]. In this case, droplets of different sizes vibrate at different speeds and collide with each other, facilitating breakage of the interfacial film and coalescence of the droplets. Therefore, low frequency ultrasound can separate droplets from the continuous phase, which is attributed to mechanical vibrations. In high frequency USWs, the primary acoustic force F 1, a drives dispersed droplets to aggregate into bandings. However, the interfacial film cannot be broken and impedes the coalescence of droplets, resulting in lower demulsification efficiency.

In fact, the mechanical vibrations generated by ultrasound over 1 MHz is significantly gentler and cannot break stable interfacial film [17,30]. However, ultrasound in MHz range is often used to establish standing wave fields to aggregate water droplets in crude oil since it provides a larger number of nodal planes [31]. This is an effective method for separating emulsions with comparatively weaker interfacial film, because the aggregation of droplets increases the probability of their collision, promoting further coalescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of energy density on emulsion separation
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of energy density is required for efficient demulsification. As shown in Fig. 3, at frequencies of 25.8, 39.4, 90.0 and 126.4 kHz, the corresponding optimal energy densities are 41.0, 38.4, 44.3 and 37.2 J·ml−1, respectively.

At frequencies of 25.8 and 39.4 kHz, mechanical vibrations induced by low frequency ultrasound cause the droplets to collide with each other, thereby promoting their coalescence. When the energy density is increased, cavitation bubbles are generated once the cavitation threshold is reached, as shown in the dotted cycles of Fig. 3. The cavitation bubbles drive the vibration of surrounding emulsions, thereby enhancing the droplet collision, as shown by Video 1 of the supplementary materials. It should be noted that this acoustic cavitation is classified as stable cavitation. The cavitation bubbles oscillate in volume during the expansion and compression cycles [32,33], which indicates that the droplets near the bubbles vibrate more vigorously. Fig. 4 shows the process of droplet coalescence near cavitation bubbles. Ultrasound with low acoustic intensity causes the bubbles to vibrate without imploding and generate shock waves [34], which can destabilize the interfacial film between the droplets. The droplets (yellow circles) gradually approach each other, collide and eventually coalesce within 304 ms. This indicates that the vibration of the cavitation bubbles facilitates droplet collision and coalescence.

When the energy density exceeds a certain value, the demulsification efficiency reaches a peak and then decreases, as shown in Fig. 3. Excessive energy density causes the implosion of cavitation bubbles, resulting in high temperature, high pressure and micro-jets [35]. Such high energy density transmitted to the droplets is sufficient to break them into smaller ones, that is, to cause secondary emulsification. Therefore, moderate acoustic cavitation where cavitation bubbles vibrate rather than implode is required to improve demulsification efficiency.

At frequencies of 90.0 and 126.4 kHz, the droplets aggregate into bandings to facilitate emulsion separation. As shown in Fig. 3, the demulsification efficiency increases with an increase of energy density, as a result of the increase in the primary acoustic force that promotes droplet migration. As shown in Fig. 5 , droplets aggregate and form bandings in high frequency USWs (also presented in Video 2 of the supplementary materials). It can be seen that most of the droplets have aggregated into bandings, while the residual tiny droplets remain stabilized in the emulsion since the primary acoustic force is weak and cannot drive their migration. A further increase in energy density causes more severe energy attenuation of acoustic wave, leading to the occurrence of acoustic steaming. The solid cycles in Fig. 3 represent the occurrence of acoustic streaming. The micro streaming disturbs droplet bandings and re-disperses the aggregated droplets, resulting in reduced demulsification efficiency.
In summary, severe acoustic cavitation at low frequencies and acoustic streaming at high frequencies should be avoided in order to achieve maximum demulsification efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of treatment time on emulsion separation
ce:section.ce:para: At the optimal active electric power, the relationship between demulsification efficiency and treatment time is shown in Fig. 6 . At 25.8 kHz, the treatment time is increased to allow the droplets to vibrate and coalesce more fully, with an optimum processing time of 15 min. However, ultrasonic irradiation increases the temperature of the emulsion, thereby accelerating the evaporation of water and producing more bubble nuclei. Further increase of the treatment time causes severe acoustic cavitation due to the large number of nuclei in the emulsion. This results in the dispersion of previously coalesced droplets, reducing demulsification efficiency. In addition, the increased number of cavitation bubbles hinders acoustic propagation in the emulsion and reduces the transmission efficiency of acoustic energy. Therefore, the treatment time should be set to an optimal value for efficient demulsification.

At 126.4 kHz, the demulsification efficiency initially increases with treatment time, stabilizing after 10 min. When the treatment time reaches 10 min, most of the droplets have aggregated into bandings. However, residual small droplets are difficult to migrate to the pressure nodes, which limits further improvement in demulsification efficiency. In this case, increasing the frequency is a promising way to increase the primary acoustic force, making the aggregation of small droplets more efficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonic emulsion separation for different emulsion properties
Effect of continuous phase viscosity on emulsion separation
ce:section.ce:para:
The high viscosity continuous phase limits droplet vibration at low frequencies and droplet migration at high frequencies, which results in lower demulsification efficiency, as shown in Fig. 7 . At 25.8 kHz, the optimal energy density increases as the continuous phase viscosity increases. The higher viscosity of the emulsion promotes negative pressure in the expansion phase of acoustic waves and increases the cavitation threshold. However, at 126.4 kHz, optimal energy density decreases with the continuous phase viscosity. According to Eq. (3), the attenuation coefficient of the acoustic energy α increases linearly with viscosity [36], indicating that acoustic streaming is more likely to occur in high-viscosity emulsions.
(3)
α
=
f 2
8
π 2
ρ o
c
o
3
4 3
μ +
μ ′
where μ and μ' are the dynamic viscosity and the bulk viscosity, respectively.

In order to evaluate the effect of ultrasonic treatment on demulsification, the relative demulsification efficiency ψ = η th/η 0 is defined, where η th is the demulsification efficiency at optimal energy density, and η 0 is the demulsification efficiency of gravitational sedimentation. As shown in Fig. 8 , at 126.4 kHz, the relative demulsification efficiency ψ increases slightly with the continuous phase viscosity, and is only 1.23 at a viscosity of 282.17 mPa·s. This indicates that the high viscosity continuous phase severely limits droplet migration in high frequency USWs. When the viscous resistance force reaches balance with the primary acoustic force, the droplet migration velocity decreases sharply, hindering droplet aggregation. Increasing the energy density is a way to improve the primary acoustic force [37,38], however, this facilitates the occurrence of acoustic streaming. At 25.8 kHz, ψ is as high as 1.98 at a viscosity of 282.17 mPa·s, which indicates that low frequency ultrasound is superior to high frequency ultrasound for separating high-viscosity emulsions. At low frequencies, the high specific energy density transmitted to the droplets causes them to vibrate, which facilitates droplet collision and coalescence into larger droplets. From the industrial point of view, both input energy and efficiency are the main parameters that affect production. Low frequency ultrasound (25.8 kHz) achieves a higher relative demulsification efficiency ψ than high frequency ultrasound (126.4 kHz) at a viscosity of 282.17 mPa·s. However, the energy density at 25.8 kHz is 5 times than at 126.4 kHz, as shown in Fig. 7. In the cases of e ≤ 20.0 J·ml−1, high frequency (126.4 kHz) presents a greater demulsification efficiency than low frequency (25.8 kHz) at a viscosity of 282.17 mPa·s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of interfacial tension on emulsion separation
ce:section.ce:para: The demulsification efficiency decreases as the interfacial tension decreases, as shown in Fig. 9 . The main reason for this is that the lower the interfacial tension, the more stable the oil–water interface, which hinders the coalescence of the droplets. At 25.8 kHz, the optimal energy density increases with the decrease of interfacial tension. This suggests that more acoustic energy is required to break the stable oil–water interface in emulsions with low interfacial tension. At 126.4 kHz, the optimal energy density remains constant for varying interfacial tensions, which also indicates that the interfacial properties have no effect on the attenuation of acoustic energy.

For a low interfacial tension of 4.79 mN·m−1, the relative demulsification efficiency ψ is 2.79 at 25.8 kHz, which is significantly higher than 1.61 at 126.4 kHz, as shown in Fig. 10 . At 25.8 kHz, however, a higher energy density should be input to the emulsion to achieve the optimal demulsification efficiency than 126.4 kHz, as shown in Fig. 9. Without breaking the droplet, a higher energy input is beneficial to break stable interface films, facilitating the coalescence of droplets. In the cases of e ≤ 60.0 J·ml−1, the demulsification efficiency at 25.8 kHz is higher than at 126.4 kHz at an interfacial tension of 4.79 mN·m−1. This indicates that low frequency ultrasound has a considerable separation effect on emulsions with stable interfacial films.

Adsorption of surfactant molecules on the oil–water interface not only reduces the interfacial tension, but also increases the interfacial film strength [39,40]. The stability of the emulsion is mainly determined by the interfacial film strength. The interfacial film with good elasticity is difficult to drain out and hinders the coalescence of droplets [41]. Mechanical vibrations induced by low frequency ultrasound can desorb surfactant molecules from the oil–water interface, i.e., surfactant redispersion occurs. Surfactant desorption reduces the interfacial film strength and destabilizes the oil–water interface, facilitating the coalescence of droplets.

In addition, as the interfacial tension increases, the difference in relative demulsification efficiency ψ at 25.8 and 126.4 kHz gradually decreases. The droplets become more prone to coalesce with the decrease of interfacial strength. In this case, shortening the spacing between the droplets is the key to improving the demulsification efficiency. Therefore, high frequency ultrasound effectively separates emulsions with high interfacial tension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dispersed phase content on emulsion separation
ce:section.ce:para: The increase of water content in oil increases the probability of droplet collision and coalescence, which results in higher demulsification efficiency, as shown in Fig. 11 . At 25.8 kHz, the optimal energy density decreases with increasing water content due to the decrease in the cavitation threshold. Since the saturated vapor pressure of water is higher than that of silicone oil at the experimental temperature, the acoustic cavitation tends to occur as the water content increases [42]. At 126.4 kHz, optimal energy density also decreases with increasing water content. Excessive droplets in oil exacerbates the attenuation of acoustic energy, facilitating the acoustic streaming.

As shown in Fig. 12 , for high water content, the relative demulsification efficiency ψ at 126.4 kHz is lower than at 25.8 kHz. However, this tendency begins to reverse as the water content decreases to 5%. This is because droplet collisions induced by mechanical vibrations are impeded due to large spacing of droplets. When the water content decreases to 2.5%, ψ is 3.8 at 126.4 kHz, which is higher than 2.9 at 25.8 kHz. As shown in Fig. 11, in the cases of e ≤ 70.0 J·ml−1, the demulsification efficiency at 126.4 kHz is higher than at 25.8 kHz at a water content interfacial tension of 2.5%. The optimal energy density at 126.4 kHz is lower than at 25.8 kHz. This indicates that high frequency ultrasound can significantly enhance the separation of the sparse water droplets from oil.

Due to the formation of droplet bandings, high frequency ultrasound favors the separation of small and sparse droplets. The primary acoustic force increases with frequency [31], resulting in higher droplet velocity. In addition, the increase of frequency can also increase the number of droplet bandings, i.e., shorten the migration distance of the droplets. Therefore, high frequency ultrasound is favorable for separating emulsions with low dispersed phase content and small droplet size.

The optimization of ultrasonic separation is investigated via microscopic and dehydration experiments, which consider comprehensively acoustic parameters and emulsion properties. The results show that optimal acoustic parameters are largely determined by the physical properties of the emulsion.

For low frequency USWs, the main cause of emulsion separation is the mechanical oscillation of the droplets. The high specific energy density is transmitted to the droplets causing them to vibrate, which not only facilitates droplet collision and coalescence, but also disperses the surfactant absorbed on the interface to decrease the interfacial strength. Therefore, low frequency ultrasound is suitable for separating emulsions with high viscosity and high interfacial strength. Compared to high frequency, however, a higher energy density should be input to the emulsion to achieve the optimal demulsification efficiency at low frequency. When the energy density is increased, cavitation bubbles are generated once the cavitation threshold is reached. Moderate cavitation bubbles can drive the surrounding emulsion to vibrate, which facilitates droplet collision and coalescence, thereby enhancing emulsion separation. However, excessive cavitation can breakup the droplets, resulting in secondary emulsification.

For high frequency USWs, droplet aggregation and bandings are the main causes of emulsion separation. The primary acoustic force drives droplets toward node planes, which facilitates droplet aggregation and coalescence. Higher frequency can result in a higher formation efficiency of droplet bandings. This can be attributed to two effects: increasing the frequency can effectively increase the primary acoustic force, thereby accelerating the migration of the droplets. In addition, the increase of frequency can also increase the number of droplet bandings, shortening the migration distance of the droplets. Therefore, high frequency ultrasound is effective in separating emulsions with low dispersed phase content and small droplet size. In addition, the results show that the optimal energy density at high frequency is lower than at low frequency. Increased the energy density is found to facilitate the aggregation of droplets, however, excessive energy density causes acoustic streaming that disturbs aggregated droplets, resulting in reduced demulsification efficiency.

Xiaoming Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - review & editing. Haiyang Gong: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Haoran Yin: Visualization, Investigation. Ziling He: Supervision. Limin He: Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51874341).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2020.105221.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms, performance optimization and new developments in demulsification processes for oil and gas applications
Adv. Colloid Interface Sci.

Chem. Eng. Res. Des.
